title: Front propagation in a nonlocal reaction-diffusion equation
speaker: Olga Turanova
affiliation: University of California, Los Angeles; Visitor, School of Mathematics
abstract: We consider a reaction-diffusion equation with a nonlocal reaction term. This PDE arises as a model in evolutionary ecology. We study the regularity properties and asymptotic behavior of its solutions.

proof_stages.label: Classical KPP benchmark for fronts
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins with the Fisher-KPP reaction-diffusion equation, the meaning of a propagating front, and the classical linear spreading speed 2√θ̄ for compactly supported data.

proof_stages.label: Nonlocal cane-toads model and basic dichotomy
proof_stages.summary: The main equation is introduced with spatial diffusion proportional to a motility trait, mutation in trait, and nonlocal competition, with finite trait intervals expected to give linear spread and unbounded trait spaces expected to accelerate despite the lack of comparison principle.

proof_stages.label: Hamilton-Jacobi route replacing comparison
proof_stages.summary: For KPP, a hyperbolic rescaling and Hopf-Cole transform lead to a limiting obstacle Hamilton-Jacobi problem whose zero set determines propagation, showing in this benchmark case that the linearized problem controls the nonlinear front.

proof_stages.label: Finite trait interval: boundedness and linear speed
proof_stages.summary: For bounded trait space, the speaker states a uniform bound on solutions, sketches the integration-in-trait/localization argument behind it, and states that Hamilton-Jacobi methods yield a linear front speed c* between the KPP speeds for the endpoint traits.

proof_stages.label: Unbounded trait space: t^{3/2} scaling and front trackers
proof_stages.summary: A heuristic combines linear growth in the motility variable with spatial diffusion √θt to predict t^{3/2} acceleration, and the quantities tracking first and last level-set crossings are defined because monotonicity of the nonlocal front is unavailable.

proof_stages.label: Local model and earlier nonlocal bounds
proof_stages.summary: The local cane-toads equation is recalled to have precise speed 4/3·t^{3/2}, while earlier results for the full nonlocal equation give an upper bound matching 4/3 and a weaker lower bound, leaving a gap and a conjecture that the nonlocal speed might also be 4/3.

proof_stages.label: New nonlocal speed and proof strategy
proof_stages.summary: The final theorem asserts an intermediate constant α* governing the nonlocal front in weaker limsup/liminf senses, thereby showing that nonlocal competition changes the speed from the local and linearized models, and the proof strategy uses rescaled problems with ρ replaced by moving indicator fronts to derive a family of Hamilton-Jacobi equations.

examples.name: Escaped-farm invasive species with linear spread
examples.note: Observed invasions after an animal escapes from a farm are cited as real-world instances where front locations grow linearly in time, illustrating the KPP benchmark.
examples.engagement_reason: escaped-farm invasions are cited only as empirical instances of linear spread

examples.name: Australian cane-toad invasion
examples.note: The accelerating invasion and faster individuals at the edge motivate introducing a motility trait, mutation in trait, and nonlocal competition, so the biological instance generates the model setup.
examples.engagement_reason: the Australian cane-toad invasion is modeled by the nonlocal PDE, whose terms and resulting accelerating front propagation are then analyzed

passes.reason: The cane-toad invasion is invoked as an accelerating spread case where the fixed-motility linear-spread model is inadequate.

the Australian cane-toad invasion is used as motivation for an accelerating nonlocal model, not as a sporadic mathematical exception or formal counterexample
It is invoked as the accelerating invasion showing that the linear-spread KPP picture is not universal.
the cane-toad invasion motivates the accelerating motility model rather than serving as a formal refutation.

examples.name: Shortest- and longest-motility KPP comparisons
examples.note: KPP equations with diffusion coefficients corresponding to the ends of the bounded trait interval explain why the bounded-trait speed lies between the shortest- and longest-leg speeds.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker applies the KPP speed formula to the shortest and longest motilities to bracket the nonlocal front speed by 2 and 2√5

passes.reason: The shortest- and longest-motility KPP equations are endpoint min/max diffusion comparisons bounding the bounded-trait speed.
